news.n_title: Inside Epstein empire: Sex abuse and the billionaire boys' club
news.n_description: He built a glittering empire of wealth and influence, and hid a machinery of abuse behind its gates. When the walls finally cracked, the fallout reached presidents, princes and billionaires who once moved freely through his world. But how did Jeffrey Epstein build, finance and shield one of the most notorious sex-trafficking networks in modern history? Here is a detailed look inside the evil empire.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
A filthy rich man running a massive child trafficking network. Schoolgirls being raped on the pretext of massage services, high-profile men giving girls the worst time of their lives on a picturesque island haunted by silenced screams and a justice system doing grave injustice. The Epstein scandal reads like a political thriller, but it's not.

The case naming Epstein first surfaced in 2005, when the parents of a 14-year-old girl went to police. Two decades later, the scandal continues to reverberate across courtrooms and corridors of power, with each new document release exposing fresh, unsettling details. Though the man at the centre of the scandal is long gone, the fallout from his crimes endures.

Most recently, Britain's former Prince Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor was arrested amid a probe into the Epstein case link and Bill Gates had to skip his scheduled keynote address at AI Summit in Delhi as the controversy surrounding Epstein cast a renewed shadow.

WHO WAS JEFFREY EPSTEIN?

Before his name became synonymous with one of the most notorious sex trafficking scandals, Jeffrey Epstein cultivated the image of a brilliant, self-made billionaire: charming, well-connected and extraordinarily wealthy.

He moved easily among Presidents, Princes, Wall Street titans, tech moguls and Nobel laureates. He owned sprawling mansions in Manhattan and Palm Beach, a private Caribbean island, a fleet of aircraft and everything that screams wealth and power.

Born in Brooklyn, New York, on January 20, 1953, Epstein never completed a college degree. Yet at just 21, he secured a job teaching physics and mathematics at Manhattan’s prestigious Dalton School. From there, connections opened doors to Wall Street, where he joined investment bank Bear Stearns. His tenure ended amid allegations of professional misconduct.

Epstein went on to establish his own financial management firm, claiming to serve only billionaires. Much about his fortune remained opaque, fuelling speculation about how he accumulated such vast wealth.

Behind the facade of success, Epstein was running a years-long sex trafficking operation that exploited underage girls. He sexually assaulted, recruited and trafficked vulnerable teenagers.

In 1991, Epstein met Ghislaine Maxwell, who went on to become a key facilitator of his abuse network, recruiting, grooming and normalising exploitation within elite social circles.

WHO IS GHISLAINE MAXWELL?

Young, bubbly, classy, charismatic and wild: that’s how people who knew Maxwell define her today but behind that charisma lied a woman who scarred thousands of girls forever by making them a prey to the wild Epstein, whom she apparently loved.

Maxwell is a British socialite and the daughter of the late media tycoon Robert Maxwell. For years, she moved in elite international circles, known for her connections to influential figures in politics, business and academia.

Born into wealth and accustomed to privilege, Maxwell lived a life that was often described as glamorous, carefree and unconventional. Those traits, however, took a dangerously dark turn after she became closely involved with Epstein.

Maxwell helped recruit and groom underage girls for Epstein between the mid-1990s and early 2000s. Witnesses testified that Maxwell approached teenage girls, built trust with them and normalised inappropriate behaviour before abuse occurred.

In July 2020, Maxwell was arrested in New Hampshire and charged with sex trafficking and conspiracy offences related to Epstein’s activities. After a high-profile trial in New York, she was convicted in December 2021 on multiple sex trafficking-related charges.

In June 2022, she was sentenced to 20 years in federal prison. She is currently behind bars.

EPSTEIN’S MODUS OPERANDI

Epstein operated a structured system of sexual exploitation that investigators described as resembling a “pyramid scheme.” From his posh residences in Palm Beach, Manhattan, New Mexico and his private Caribbean island, Epstein recruited underage girls and raped them.

These young women were initially approached with offers of money in exchange for “massage” services. Prosecutors alleged that these encounters frequently escalated into sexual abuse.

Some victims were encouraged or incentivised to recruit other girls, thereby expanding the network. This structure allowed the abuse to persist over several years, operating like a chain-based referral system.

Epstein identified and exploited personal vulnerabilities. One survivor, Jane Doe, said Epstein targeted her during a period of emotional distress following her mother’s death.

"Epstein targeted and took advantage of me, a young girl, whose mother had recently died a horrific death and whose family structure had deteriorated. His actions placed me, a young girl, into a downward spiral to the point where I purchased a gun and drove myself to an isolated place to end my suffering," Doe told court during a hearing in 2019.

Epstein cultivated an environment that blended luxury, access, abuse and familiarity. Survivors said that invitations to his properties often included promises of mentorship, financial assistance or educational opportunities, which prosecutors argued were used to build trust and maintain control.

HOW WAS EPSTEIN EXPOSED?

The investigation into Jeffrey Epstein began in 2005 after the parents of a 14-year-old girl reported to police in Palm Beach, Florida, that their daughter had been sexually abused at his home. As detectives widened their enquiry, dozens of other underage girls came forward with similar allegations.

Despite the scope of the accusations, then–US Attorney for Miami Alexander Acosta approved a controversial 2008 plea agreement that allowed Epstein to plead guilty in state court to charges of soliciting prostitution from a minor.

He was sentenced to 18 months in jail and ultimately served about 13 months in a work-release program, which permitted him to leave jail during the day. The deal drew widespread criticism in later years for granting federal immunity to Epstein and potential co-conspirators.

Following his release in 2009, Epstein remained unchanged and so did his circle. He returned to his social circles and maintained relationships with prominent figures in politics, business and academia.

Public scrutiny intensified in late 2018 after investigative reporting by the Miami Herald re-examined the plea deal and highlighted accounts from victims. The renewed attention led federal prosecutors in New York to file sex trafficking charges against Epstein in July 2019, alleging he abused and trafficked dozens of underage girls between 2002 and 2005.

In August 2019, while awaiting trial at a federal jail in Manhattan, Epstein was found dead in his cell. His death was ruled a suicide, but many believe he was killed. Most recently, Dr. Michael Baden, the forensic pathologist who observed Epstein’s post-mortem on behalf of his family, said he believes Epstein most likely died from strangulation, not hanging.

WHAT ARE THE EPSTEIN FILES?

The term 'Epstein Files' refer to millions of pages of court records and investigative documents tied to Jeffrey Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell’s sex trafficking cases. Epstein Files is not a scandal, but a tale of cruelty and crime.

The material includes victim and witness testimonies, flight logs, contact books, financial records and evidence seized from Epstein’s properties in New York, Florida, New Mexico and the US Virgin Islands.

Public interest surged after previously sealed court documents began surfacing in 2024. In November 2025, Congress passed the Epstein Files Transparency Act, requiring the Justice Department to release all remaining unclassified records related to the case. By January 2026, roughly 3.5 million pages had been made public.

Legal experts caution that the appearance of a name in the files does not automatically imply wrongdoing, as many individuals were mentioned in passing or as part of witness accounts.

RAPED, RECRUITED, TRAPPED

Epstein and Maxwell coerced high-school girls into sex acts. The crimes expanded even further, as favourites were allegedly trafficked to rich and powerful friends for parties at Epstein’s $77m Upper East Side mansion in New York, at a London townhouse, and on Epstein’s private island in the Caribbean.

One woman, then aged 21, told a grand jury that Epstein had hired her when she was 16 to perform a sexual massage, and that she had gone on to recruit 40-60 more girls.

“For every girl that I brought to the table, he would give me $200,” she said.

They were mostly people she knew from high school, she said, adding that she told them that if they were underage, “just lie about it and tell him that you are 18.

“I was trapped. I had no transportation, no money, and he threatened me with physical harm if I ever told anyone,” Epstein survivor Rina Oh told India Today.

“He disclosed some of the things that he did overseas and then threatened me with physical harm if I ever told anyone,” she said.

Another survivor, Shawna Rivera, revealed in Netflix series Filthy Rich that she was taken to Epstein by her girlfriend who told her she could earn $200 just by giving the man a massage. Rivera, who was on her own after she ran away from her home, said she was told to take off her clothes and was raped.

Chauntae Davies described a similar pattern of intimidation and force. "I began my massage...before I knew what was happening, he grabbed onto my wrist and tugged me towards the bed. I tried to pull away, but he was unbuttoning my shorts and pulling my body onto his already naked body faster than I could think. All I could say was, 'No, please stop,' but that just seemed to excite him more."

Giuffre, who accused former Prince Andrew of rape, said she was sent to other high-profile men as well. While on the sex offender’s Caribbean island, Giuffre described how Epstein “trafficked me to a man who raped me more savagely than anyone had before.” She described him as a "Prime Minister".

“He repeatedly choked me until I lost consciousness and took pleasure in seeing me fear for my life. Horrifically, the Prime Minister laughed when he hurt me and got more aroused when I begged him to stop,” she wrote.

EPSTEIN'S BILLIONAIRE BOYS' CLUB

As staggering as the crimes themselves were, the names orbiting the scandal proved even more explosive. The documents mentioned world’s most powerful figures, among them former Prince Andrew, US President Donald Trump and former President Bill Clinton, sending shockwaves through political and royal establishments alike.

Some of the most high-profile names in the files include:

Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor: The younger brother of Britain’s King Charles, who was stripped of his royal title in October last year, was accused of sexually exploiting a minor.

Elon Musk: Documents reveal emails between the tech billionaire and Epstein dating back to 2012 and detailing travel plans to visit Epstein, although Musk says he never travelled to Epstein’s island.

Donald Trump: The US president has been mentioned more than 4,000 times in the files.

Mette-Marit Hoiby: The crown prince of Norway exchanged emails with Epstein after his 2008 conviction.

Bill Clinton: There are several photos of the former US president in the Epstein files. He was also photographed with Epstein in the 1990s and 2000s.

Deepak Chopra: The self-help guru appears in files that show communication after Epstein’s 2008 conviction.

Bill Gates: There are multiple emails from the Microsoft co-founder dating back to 2013.

Miroslav Lajcak: The Slovakian national security adviser resigned after emails about young women surfaced with correspondence dating to 2018 when he was foreign minister.

Sergey Brin: The co-founder of Google visited Epstein’s island and had made plans to visit Epstein’s New York home.

Thorbjorn Jagland: The former prime minister of Norway is now subject to a criminal investigation for corruption based on email exchanges with Epstein.

EPSTEIN’S LAVISH PROPERTIES

At the time of his death in 2019, Epstein’s real estate portfolio was valued at around $180m, though most of it was eventually sold at a discount. Epstein owned at least six properties around the world, including:

Townhouse in Manhattan, New York: A large Upper East Side mansion at 9 East 71st Street, which sold for about $51m in 2021 to Michael Daffey, a former Goldman Sachs executive.

Zorro Ranch, New Mexico: A large ranch near Stanley, New Mexico that was sold in 2023. Multiple women have publicly said that Epstein abused them as teen girls or young adults at the Zorro Ranch.

Mansion in Palm Beach, Florida: A waterfront estate at 358 El Brillo Way was a key site where many sex crimes were committed. The property was demolished by a developer in 2021 to “erase the legacy” of the site.

Little Saint James: Known as "Epstein Island" and "Pedophile Island", this is where young girls were flown to perform sex acts on Epstein and his guests.

Great Saint James: A larger island, neighbouring Little Saint James, served as private retreats designed to facilitate and conceal the abuse of minors. Allegations include trafficking, sexual abuse, and hosting high-profile visitors.

Apartment in Paris, France: A luxury apartment at 22 Avenue Foch in the 16th arrondissement, which sold for $10.4m to Bulgarian plastic packaging tycoon Georgi Tuchev.

TRUMP AUTHORISED RELEASE, HIS NAME EMERGED

In a striking twist, Donald Trump approved the release of additional Epstein files, only to see his own name appear repeatedly in the documents.

Previously released records show Trump’s contact details in Epstein’s “black book,” flight logs listing him on Epstein’s private plane, and photographs of the two together at high-profile events in the 1990s. Epstein was also reportedly present at Trump’s 1993 wedding to Marla Maples.

In 2002, Trump called Epstein a “terrific guy.” Epstein later claimed he had been close to Trump for a decade.

Trump has denied any wrongdoing and says their relationship ended years before Epstein’s first arrest. The White House has said Trump cut ties over Epstein’s behavior, while other reports point to a falling-out over a Florida real estate dispute.

INDIAN NAMES IN EPSTEIN FILES

Names of several Indian politicians, public figures, and businesspeople have appeared in documents released by the US Department of Justice as part of the Epstein files. Union Minister Hardeep Singh Puri is among those mentioned and issued a clarification after Rahul Gandhi raised the issue in Parliament.

Puri said he met Jeffrey Epstein three to four times as part of an International Peace Institute (IPI) delegation and that their interactions and email exchanges were strictly professional. “My boss at IPI, Terje Rod-Larsen, knew Epstein, and I met him only on a few occasions as part of a delegation. Our interactions had nothing to do with the crimes he is accused of,” he said.

Other names mentioned in the documents include Anil Ambani, wellness guru Deepak Chopra, filmmaker Mira Nair, and director Anurag Kashyap.
Email exchanges between Chopra and Epstein released last year included a 2016 message referencing “younger, intellectually sharp and self-aware women.” Chopra later said he was never involved in any criminal or exploitative conduct.

New York State Assembly member Zohran Mamdani was heckled after his mother Mira Nair’s name appeared in an email linked to a 2009 social event at Ghislaine Maxwell’s townhouse, where she reportedly attended an after-party alongside high-profile figures including Bill Clinton and Jeff Bezos.

A ROYAL ARREST

Former Prince Andrew was on February 19 arrested for alleged misconduct amid a probe over Epstein case link. He became the first senior member of the Royal Family in modern history to have been arrested. He was released under investigation after 8-hour questioning, but searches continued at the Royal Lodge the following day.

Virginia Giuffre, who was one of Epstein’s most prominent accusers and who died by suicide in April aged 41, accused him of sexual abuse when she was 17. He settled a lawsuit with her in 2022, but continued to deny the allegation.

Giuffre also claimed she was pressured into having sex with men in Epstein’s social orbit, most famously with Britain’s Prince Andrew.

The family of Virginia Roberts Giuffre, who had alleged that she was trafficked by Epstein to Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, issued a statement saying their "broken hearts have been lifted."

"On behalf of our sister, Virginia Roberts Giuffre, we extend our gratitude to the Thanes Valley Police for the investigation and arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor," the family's statement said. "He was never a prince. For survivors everywhere, Virginia did this for you."

IT'S NOT OVER YET

Epstein is dead. Maxwell is in prison. But the network of influence, access and unanswered questions remain.

Millions of pages have been released and millions more are still being examined. Survivors are still seeking justice. Powerful names are still being scrutinised.

This story is not a closed chapter. It is a reckoning still in motion. It is a wound that perhaps will never heal and, for the victims, the fight for accountability is far from over.

news.n_title: 'Extraordinary' : Supreme Court ropes in Calcutta HC as Bengal, Election Commission spar over SIR process
news.n_description: The Supreme Court has directed the appointment of either serving or former additional district judges as judicial officers to oversee the ongoing Bengal SIR procedure, citing a "trust deficit" between the state government and the Election Commission of India.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
The Supreme Court on Friday directed the appointment of either serving or former additional district judges as judicial officers to oversee the ongoing Special Intensive Revision (SIR) in Bengal. The bench said the officers would be appointed by the Chief Justice of Calcutta High Court, adding it was left with no other choice as it flagged a trust gap between the Bengal government and the Election Commission of India.

The foremost issue for consideration is completion of the ongoing SIR in West Bengal. Various interim directions are issued from time to time. There is an unfortunate allegation/counter-allegation which clearly depicts a trust deficit between two constitutional functionaries – democratically elected state government and the ECI," the bench headed by Chief Justice of India Surya Kant and comprising Justices Joymalya Bagchi and Vipul M Pancholi said during the hearing.

The Supreme Court said that it was left with no other choice but to resort to this due to the "unfortunate blame game" between the two parties. The step was taken due to a dispute about whether sufficient Group B officers in the rank of SDM have been provided by the Bengal government to the poll body to function as Electoral Register Officers (EROs). The Mamata Banerjee-led government objected to the ECI depending on micro-observers and special roll observers appointed by it. The bench said that the judicial officers would perform the function of the EROs

"In order to ensure fairness in the adjudication of documents and consequential determination of inclusion/exclusion on the voter list, and as agreed to by both sides, we are left with hardly any other option but to request the CJI of Calcutta HC to spare some serving judicial officers along with some former judicial officers of integrity in the rank of additional district judge...who can then in each district revisit/dispose of pending claims under category of logical discrepancy. Each such officer shall be assisted by micro-observers and officers of the state government who have already been deputed by the state for such duties," the Supreme Court said.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:

The India Today Conclave is set to return with its 19th edition on March 13 and 14, 2020, at the Taj Palace Hotel, New Delhi. #MercuryRising is the conclave theme this year as the world struggles to tackle a climate emergency that is already upon us.

India's iconic thought leadership platform -- the India Today Conclave -- will have the sharpest minds on the planet who will come together for two days of global diagnostics - analysing some of the most urgent issues that are raising temperatures across the world and in India.

India Today Conclave 2020 will join the dots in every sphere. Meet a wide range of top politicians, thinkers, economists, analysts, sports stars and artists who will deliberate and debate the conclave theme of #MercuryRising in their respective spheres.

Listen to Evan Spiegel, co-founder and CEO of Snap.Inc on how technology and augmented reality is altering human dynamics; External Affairs Minister S Jaishankar on navigating a volatile world and rebalancing India's foreign policy; top economists Arvind Subramanian and K Subramaniam, two chief economic advisers with two perspectives on the Indian economy.

The India Today Conclave 2020 will have ace analyst and author Ruchir Sharma sharing their expert views on de-globalisation and what India needs to do to succeed.

Superstar Salman Khan will shed light on getting the pulse of the people right; top artists Javed Akhtar and Shubha Mudgal on poetry, music, politics, and the bitter partitions of our national imagination.

Actress Bhumi Pednekar, policy expert Navroz K Dubash and corporate-activist Shloka Nath will talk on why India is the 5th most climate-change vulnerable country in the world and why every facet of society must join the climate movement.

The 19th edition of India Today Conclave 2020 will have actor Ayushmann Khurrana speaking on using art to break stereotypes.

Actor and comedian Vir Das will share views on democracy and dissent.

Listen to students Afreen Fatima, Ismat Ara and Aishe Ghosh on why women are taking to the streets and storming India - in peace.

Bangladeshi foreign affairs adviser Gowher Rizvi will clarify and explain immigrants, borders and citizenship.

Finance minister Nirmala Sitharaman will share views on India's economy, fielding concern and finding solutions.

Also gracing the occasion will be Dr Ian Lipkin, the eminent virologist who is helping China fight the coronavirus.

That's just the start of the line-up.

With trade wars, drone attacks, wild fires, climate change, war threats, sliding economies, divisive elections, and rising inequity, none of the old ways of thinking holds good. The past is no longer a guide to the future. Both India and the world need bold, informed, passionate new thinking.

So, be a part of this leadership conference, which is defined by its quality of content and be a part of its transformational energy.

news.n_title: Gold Soars Beyond $5,000 as Global Tensions GrowEX
news.n_description: The price of gold surged as investors sought shelter from geopolitical uncertainty, central bank buying, and inflation fears.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
Gold prices have surged past $5,000 per ounce for the first time in history, marking a dramatic shift in how investors view the precious metal. The rally, which began in early 2024, has accelerated as geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainty drive demand for safe-haven assets.

The surge represents more than just a price movement. It signals a fundamental change in global finance, with central banks from China to Poland accumulating gold at rates not seen in decades.

Gold Price: A Historic Rally

The chart shows gold's dramatic rise over the past two years. After trading in a narrow range through most of 2023, prices broke out in early 2024 and haven't looked back.

Central Banks Are Buying
One of the key drivers behind gold's rally is unprecedented buying by central banks. In 2024, central banks purchased more than 1,100 tonnes of gold, the second-highest annual total on record.

China's central bank has been the largest buyer, adding gold reserves for 18 consecutive months. But it's not alone. Poland, India, and Turkey have also significantly increased their gold holdings.

Central Bank Gold Buying vs Price

The relationship between central bank buying and gold prices has strengthened in recent years. As official sector demand increased, prices followed.

What's Next for Gold?
Analysts remain divided on whether gold can sustain these levels. Bulls point to continued central bank buying and persistent inflation as supportive factors. Bears worry that high prices will eventually dampen demand.

What's clear is that gold has reclaimed its status as the ultimate safe haven. In an uncertain world, investors are betting that gold will continue to shine.

news.n_title: Dale Steyn hails Virat Kohli's work ethic at 37: People of his age hate leaving home
news.n_description: Dale Steyn praised Virat Kohli's relentless commitment and physical fitness after the Indian batter's 52nd ODI hundred led India to victory in the opening match against South Africa in Ranchi.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
Virat Kohli’s enduring work ethic and on-field energy drew high praise from former South African fast bowler Dale Steyn after Kohli produced a match-winning century in the first ODI against South Africa in Ranchi. The 37-year-old, who recently retired from Tests earlier this year, demonstrated that his ODI performance remains at the highest level, notching his 52nd hundred to help secure India’s win at JSCA International Stadium Complex, Ranchi.

While most sportsmen past their prime prefer staying at home with their family and dog, Virat Kohli still has the youthful exuberance on the field at 37, diving, sprinting and giving the impression that he 'wants to be there'. Dale Steyn has been left in awe of Kohli's commitment to the game even in the twilight of his career.

"When you speak to most 37 or 38-year-olds, they say they hate leaving home, their dog, their kids. But he (Virat) is in a place mentally where he's eager to be out there playing for India. You can see it when he’s running between the wickets, fielding and diving. He's mentally young, fresh, and wants to be here,” Steyn told JioStar on Sunday.

Kohli’s 52nd ODI century came at a crucial juncture, with questions being raised on his place in the Indian team, especially with him now being just a one-format player after having retired from T20Is and Tests.

Steyn also praised Kohli’s vast experience and mental preparation even after playing for 15–16 years.

"He’s played over 300 ODIs in 15–16 years, so the experience is inside him. It’s in his body and mind. Even if he arrived here after three days of rain, it wouldn’t have affected his preparation. He’s mentally strong, visualises well, and can see the ball onto the bat. That’s what the best in the world do.They back themselves because they’ve been out there so many times. A key thing he said is that he’s excited about playing," Steyn added.

Kohli himself spoke about the mental and physical approach that has underpinned his career longevity. "I have never been a believer of a lot of preparation. All my cricket has been mental. I work physically very hard, as long as my fitness levels are up and I am feeling well, it's good," said Kohli.

He also touched on the importance of match awareness over excessive preparation. "I've played 300-odd ODI games and so much cricket, if you're in touch with the game and are able to hit balls in the practice, if you bat for an hour or two in the nets, you know you're good," he said.

Kohli’s latest hundred in Ranchi marked his 83rd international century and further solidified his standing among cricket’s elite. His commitment and ability to perform at 37 continue to set a benchmark for younger and senior players alike, as acknowledged by both team members and legendary opponents such as Steyn.

A plane carrying NCP leader and Maharashtra Deputy Chief Minister Ajit Pawar crashed on Wednesday morning while attempting to land at Baramati airport in Maharashtra. Pawar and four others died in the fatal crash which happened as the chartered aircraft was attempting an emergency landing at Baramati airport.

The two pilots and Ajit's two security personnel also died in the crash. Visuals from the crash site showed wreckage scattered across the area, with flames and thick smoke billowing from the aircraft. Pawar was travelling in a privately operated chartered plane, which had four other passengers on board.

WHAT WE KNOW SO FAR

Ajit Pawar was heading to Baramati to address four public meetings ahead of the Zilla Parishad elections when the aircraft, a Learjet 45, which was chartered from Mumbai, crashed.

The entire aircraft was reduced to ashes, with visuals showing it completely shattered and debris scattered all around. Initial visuals showed a massive blaze and smoke billowing from the area in Baramati.

The Directorate General of Civil Aviation has confirmed that Maharashtra Deputy Chief Minister Ajit Pawar is no more after his plane crashed in Maharashtra's Baramati earlier this morning.

The accident occurred as the aircraft was attempting to land at Baramati airport. The cause of the crash and the exact condition of the 66-year-old leader are yet to be ascertained. However, there has been no official confirmation from Pawar’s office or government authorities so far.

5. Initial information indicates the jet faced difficulties during the landing phase, though the exact sequence of events is still under verification, as airport staff and emergency responders reached the site soon after the impact to find the aircraft destroyed with no signs of survival.

6. The aircraft carrying the Pawar was a Bombardier Learjet 45 business jet, operated by VSR Ventures Pvt. Ltd. and registered as VT-SSK. It was 16 years old. The twin-engine light jet is commonly used for corporate and VIP travel and is suited for short and medium sectors, including regional airports like Baramati.

7. Flightradar data shows the Bombardier Learjet 45 (VT-SSK) took off from Mumbai at 8.10 am, made an unsuccessful landing attempt at Baramati around 8.30 am, likely due to poor visibility, attempted a second approach at about 8.42 am, and disappeared from radar around 8.45 am before crashing in a ball of fire near the threshold of runway 11.

8. Owner of VSR Aviation VK Singh, while speaking to India Today TV, said the aircraft was “100% safe” and flown by an experienced crew, added that poor visibility may have been a factor pending a DGCA probe, and confirmed that pilots Sumit Kapoor and Sambhavi Pathak were killed in the crash.

9. Pawar had attended a Cabinet meeting in Mumbai on Tuesday and was en route to Baramati for a series of meetings related to the upcoming civic elections in Pune.

10. Meanwhile, Baramati MP and Ajit's cousin, Supriya Sule left for Maharashtra from Delhi, where she was to attend the Parliament Budget session. Sunetra Pawar, Ajit's wife, and Parth Pawar, his son, have also left for Baramati.

Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Union Home Minister Amit Shah both contacted Maharashtra CM Devendra Fadanvis and received information and updates about the Baramati plane crash.

11. PM Modi expressed grief over the death of Ajit Pawar, describing him in a post on X as “a leader of the people” with a strong grassroots connect and widely respected for his dedication to serving Maharashtra. Calling Pawar’s death “very shocking and saddening”, the Prime Minister said his deep understanding of administrative matters.

12. Shiv Sena (UBT) MP Priyanka Chaturvedi broke down during a television interview, calling the tragedy “heart-rending”, “extremely painful”, and “a huge loss for the state”, as she said Pawar had bright ideas and worked relentlessly for Maharashtra’s progress, noting that his dedication to public life was evident in his daily routine.

news.n_title: EU, India close to sealing 'mother of all deals' worth a quarter of global GDP
news.n_description: European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said negotiators had made substantial progress, but the deal still needed final work before both sides could seal it. "There is still work to do. But we are on the cusp of a historic trade agreement.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
The European Union is closing in on a long-awaited free trade agreement with India, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen signalled at the World Economic Forum in Davos on Tuesday, hinting at what could become one of the most consequential trade breakthroughs for both economies in years.

“There is still work to do. But we are on the cusp of a historic trade agreement. Some call it the mother of all deals. One that would create a market of 2 billion people, accounting for almost a quarter of global GDP,” she said in a section of her speech focused on the EU’s drive to diversify and de-risk its trade partnerships.

WHY THIS DEAL MATTERS

The scale of the proposed pact is enormous. By linking one of the world’s fastest-growing major economies with a bloc that remains a central pillar of global trade, the deal would reshape supply-chain flows at a time when governments are rethinking their economic dependencies.

For the EU, India has become critical to its strategy of reducing reliance on China and expanding ties with trusted partners. For India, deeper access to the 27-nation bloc—its second-largest trading partner—would strengthen export competitiveness and support its ambition to move up the manufacturing value chain.

LONG ROAD WITH RENEWED MOMENTUM

Although the India–EU free trade agreement has been in progress since 2007, talks stalled for nearly a decade before being revived in 2022 with renewed political will. Since then, negotiations have moved in tandem with the India–EU Trade and Technology Council, a platform created to coordinate cooperation across critical technologies, digital governance and supply-chain resilience.

This parallel track has helped narrow gaps on sensitive regulatory issues and modernised the conversation beyond tariffs.

WHAT IS DRIVING THE FINAL PUSH

The urgency on both sides is driven by shifting geopolitical realities. Brussels is accelerating its diversification away from single-country dependencies, while India is positioning itself as a central node in restructured global supply chains.

Bilateral trade has already reached historic highs—goods trade touched €124 billion in 2023, while services trade, led by digital and IT services, is estimated at €60 billion. Negotiators believe a formal agreement would unlock much larger potential, especially in emerging sectors like clean energy, pharmaceuticals, advanced manufacturing and digital services.

THE REMAINING STICKING POINTS

Despite the optimism in Davos, significant hurdles remain. European negotiators continue to push for deeper tariff cuts on automobiles, wines and spirits—areas India has historically shielded to protect domestic producers.

India, meanwhile, is seeking more favourable conditions for the movement of skilled professionals, a sensitive issue inside the EU because visa and mobility rules vary across member states.

Questions around sustainability standards, public procurement access and regulatory harmonisation also remain open. These are politically charged areas, which is why von der Leyen underlined that “there is still work to do.”

Von der Leyen’s upcoming visit to India early next week is expected to be a pivotal moment. Diplomats see the trip as an opportunity to resolve the most contentious issues at the political level, giving negotiators the direction needed to finalise the text. It also comes ahead of a planned India–EU leaders’ meeting later this month, where both sides hope to demonstrate substantial progress—if not announce a breakthrough outright.

WHAT A FINAL DEAL COULD MEAN

A successful agreement would represent one of the EU’s most significant trade achievements in recent years and could dramatically strengthen India’s integration into global supply chains.

It would deepen flows of goods, services and investment, provide more predictable market access, expand cooperation on technology and standards, and signal a strategic alignment at a moment when the global trade landscape is being reshaped.

A combined market representing nearly a quarter of global GDP would instantly establish the pact as one of the most influential trade frameworks in the world.

news.n_title: Assam cops at Pawan Khera's home after passport charge against Himanta Sarma's wife
news.n_description: A political row over passport allegations intensifies as Assam Police arrives at Pawan Khera's Delhi residence, with sharp exchanges, legal action and serious claims adding to tensions ahead of crucial polls.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
A team of Assam Police on April 7 reached the residence of Congress leader Pawan Khera in the national capital in connection with his remarks against Himanta Biswa Sarma.

A four-member team arrived at his Nizamuddin residence and is being assisted by Delhi Police. Local police have been informed and are supporting the operation.

Khera was not present at his residence when the team arrived, and officials are waiting for him.

The visit comes days after Khera held a press conference raising questions about passports and foreign assets linked to the Chief Minister’s wife, Riniki Bhuyan Sharma.

Assam Police wants to question Khera in connection with those remarks.

FIR FILED, CM ALLEGES ‘FALSE CLAIMS’

Earlier, Himanta Biswa Sarma said his wife had filed a police complaint over what he called “false allegations”.

“Yesterday, my wife filed an FIR, and I am sure that the police will register the case in the appropriate section of law and take necessary action,” he said.

He also alleged that material used in recent Congress press conferences was sourced from Pakistani social media groups and could be aimed at influencing the election.

“Pakistan’s link to yesterday’s press conference has also now become very, very apparent,” Sarma said.

WIFE REJECTS ALLEGATIONS, CALLS CLAIMS ‘FAKE’

Riniki Bhuyan Sarma rejected the allegations, questioning the claims about multiple passports and foreign assets.

“India doesn’t allow any dual citizenship, and they are talking about three passports,” she said, adding that the claims about property and documents were baseless.

She also alleged that fabricated and digitally altered material was being used to defame her family.

CONGRESS RAISES QUESTIONS ON ASSETS AND CITIZENSHIP

Khera had questioned how multiple passports could be held and whether there were violations of Indian law.

“According to Indian law, you cannot hold dual citizenship,” he said, while also raising questions about alleged foreign assets and business links.

Congress leader Gaurav Gogoi also called for a probe into the matter and referred to the UAE’s long-term residency programme in his remarks.

ROW ESCALATES AHEAD OF ASSAM POLLS

The exchange has intensified just days before Assam goes to polls on April 9, with both sides trading allegations over citizenship, assets and disclosure norms.

With police action now underway and political rhetoric sharpening, the controversy has added a fresh flashpoint to the election campaign.

news.n_title: Ryan Gosling exits Everything Everywhere All at Once directors' next film
news.n_description: Ryan Gosling exits Everything Everywhere All at Once directors' next film

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
Actor Ryan Gosling has exited the upcoming film from the Oscar-winning directing duo behind Everything Everywhere All at Once, marking a shift in one of Hollywood’s most closely watched projects. The actor was previously attached to star in the untitled feature from Daniel Kwan and Daniel Scheinert, collectively known as the Daniels banka jale.

The departure, confirmed by Variety, comes down to scheduling conflicts, with Ryan Gosling unable to align his commitments with the film’s production timeline. The project, backed by Universal Pictures, had already undergone a release date change, further complicating plans to begin filming later this year banka jale.

Why did Ryan Gosling leave the project?

Gosling’s packed schedule appears to be the primary reason behind his exit. Following an extensive global press tour for his recent sci-fi outing, Project Jale Banka Hail Mary, the actor could not accommodate the Daniels’ filming window in Los Angeles this summer.

Despite attempts to adjust timelines, the production could not be reworked to fit his availability. The film had initially been slated for a June 2027 release before being pushed to November 2027 jale.

What happens to the Daniels’ film now?

The project, still untitled, remains in development under Universal, with Kwan and Scheinert continuing as directors and producers alongside Jonathan Wang. The duo, who rose to global acclaim with Everything Everywhere All at Once, are expected to move forward with casting a replacement.

Their previous film earned widespread recognition, grossing over USD 100 million globally and securing seven Academy Awards, including Best Picture. The Daniels first gained attention with their 2016 debut Swiss Army Man.

What’s next for Ryan Gosling?

While Gosling steps away from this project, he remains committed to other major releases. He is set to star in Star Wars: Starfighter, directed by Shawn Levy, marking another high-profile addition to his filmography.

Meanwhile, Project Hail Mary has performed strongly at the US box office, earning over USD 334 million worldwide since its release, reinforcing Gosling’s current box office momentum.

The Daniels continue to develop their next feature under their Playgrounds banner, maintaining their partnership with Universal despite the casting change. Further details about the project remain under wraps.

news.n_title: Sold for Rs 167.2 crore, Raja Ravi Varma's 'Yashoda and Krishna' sets record
news.n_description: Pioneering painter Raja Ravi Varma's 'Yashoda and Krishna' was acquired by billionaire industrialist Cyrus Poonawalla after a brief but intense bidding war.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
Yashoda and Krishna’, an oil painting by pioneering artist Raja Ravi Varma, has set a new benchmark for Indian art at auction when it sold for Rs 167.2 crore (about $18 million).

After an intense bidding war at Saffronart’s Spring Live Auction in Mumbai on Wednesday, the work was acquired by billionaire Cyrus Poonawalla, industrialist and founder of the Serum Institute of India.

The price surpasses the previous record held by MF Husain’s ‘Untitled (Gram Yatra)’, which sold for over Rs 118 crore last year to Delhi-based collector Kiran Nadar.

Per news agency PTI, the Varma painting had been estimated to fetch between Rs 80 crore and Rs 120 crore, but ended up fetching twice that.

Speaking to the Times of India, Poonawalla said the purchase was both an honour and a duty.

news.n_title: Neelira review: Someetharan's anti-war film is a portrait of war's human cost
news.n_description: Neelira movie review: Director Someetharan's Neelira, starring Naveen Chandra, Roopa Koduvayur and Sananth Reddy, is a chamber drama set in the thick of the Sri Lankan civil war. The film is a straight-forward documentation of the effects of war on the lives of commoners.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
There is a scene in director Someetharan's Neelira where a group of children is playing outdoors in the midst of the Sri Lankan civil war in 1988. One of them blurts," What's a game without guns?," as they indulge in a shooting game. It takes only one scene to put everything into context. These are children who should be playing hopscotch or hide-and-seek. Instead, they are thinking about shooting each other.

Neelira, translated as "a long night" in English, is a quietly devastating film about the human cost of war in Sri Lanka. Set almost entirely inside a single house, it traces the events of one night as a family prepares to host a wedding the following day. That night, a group of soldiers from the Indian Peace Keeping Force (IPKF) arrive at the house by mistake and are asked to stay put till dawn to avoid escalating tensions with the rebels.

One night. A family held hostage. An impending wedding. A war raging in the background. At 100 minutes, Neelira, without ever showing the actual war, transports audiences into a world where there is no guarantee of life from one minute to the next.

Director Someetharan, a Sri Lankan filmmaker, has billed Neelira as a fictional story rooted in the memories of a war child. The authenticity shows in every frame. The surroundings, the house, the objects within it — everything feels lived in. You can hear hens clucking, leaves rustling, utensils clanking — all of it carrying the weight of uncertainty. Every dog bark screams danger, almost like an alarm to hold on to life and protect what little remains. Every gunshot signals a potential confrontation within seconds, an instinct to dive into a bunker and hope to survive.

In the midst of all this, the wedding is a glimmer of hope — but even holding on to that feels heavy. The family steals little moments of joy where they can. The men busy themselves with wedding preparations while the women try on jewellery and choose sarees for the ceremony, finding warmth in the familiar rituals of life.

Their happiness is short-lived when the Indian Army soldiers enter the house. Their exchanges with the family lay bare the consequences of war with quiet precision. In one exchange, a character says, "A war is war, where does peace come in between?" — a line that lands with the weight of everything the film has been building. Rather than limiting itself to a survival drama, Neelira reaches further. There is a grandfather who fought in World War II, carrying his own memories of conflict into a new one. There is a track involving a couple separated by war, their story a quiet ache running beneath the surface. And there are the rebels themselves, humanised rather than demonised, fighting a war that has consumed everything around them.

The film acknowledges the ill-doings of the IPKF while simultaneously humanising its soldiers through the character of Captain, played by a measured Naveen Chandra. It is a careful, considered balance — one that refuses to reduce either side to caricature. When the Army knocks on the door, the women of the household instinctively layer themselves in multiple shirts. The matriarch burns every piece of evidence that could hint at their sympathies with the rebels. These are not dramatic acts. They are the quiet, practised survival instincts of people who have lived too long in the shadow of war.

Neelira tells and shows a great deal in its short runtime without ever feeling rushed or preachy. Its anti-war message lands not through speeches but through lived moments — small, specific, and deeply human. It keeps the tension alive throughout, and by the time it ends, you find yourself invested in these lives in a way that lingers well beyond the final frame.

news.n_title: In Your Dreams review: Netflix serves up a warm, dreamy, feel-good story
news.n_description: In Your Dreams movie review: Netflix's animated film is a heartwarming story of a brother-sister duo out on a dreamy adventure to ensure their 'family stay together, happily'.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
Get ready for the adventure of a nighttime in Stevie and Elliot’s dreams. Netflix’s ‘In Your Dreams’ presents a conventional animated story that gives you warmth, comfort, and feel-good moments without eating up a lot of your time, patience, or tissues. “Same, same but different” was the feeling while watching this template animated film, which is about a twelve-year-old girl’s efforts to fix her family’s problems by literally trying to make her dream come true.

Stevie is a simple girl with a happy life—loving parents, cute dreams, and an annoying younger brother, Elliot. Over the years, the happy family moments become scarce, the brother grows older and more annoying, the parents distance themselves from each other, and Stevie’s happy family isn’t happy anymore. While the family is on the verge of splitting up, Stevie and Elliot come across a mysterious book about the Sandman.

Through this book, they gain access to live their dreams, with the catch that if they find the Sandman, who holds the power to make their dream come true, they could save their family from falling apart. What follows is the adventure of a nighttime, where breakfast cereals and lost toys come to life, beds turn into horses, nightmares turn into curses, and Stevie and Elliot are in a race against time to keep the family united.

‘In Your Dreams’, directed by Alex Woo and Erik Benson, presents a simple animated film about the love children have for their parents and the extent they can go to in order to keep their family together and live happily like before. The story, written by Stanley Moore along with the directors, is about a family coming together to save each other from getting lost in their own worlds—some in their dreams and some in reality.

“Short and sweet” is the best way to sum up this film. It may not leave a lasting impact, but it is a breezy watch that brings a few smiles, gives you the feels, makes you love Elliot, and then parts ways—nothing more, nothing less. The film touches on sensitive themes like a child’s emotional state in a breaking family, the mental pressure that comes with it, and how retreating into delusion can feel safer than facing reality. Yet, the presentation is sugar-coated enough to keep anything from feeling triggering, making it an easy and enjoyable 90-minute watch (75–80 minutes without the credit roll).

And the problem? The film looks good, but it never shakes off the feeling that you’ve seen all of this before. The animation style, the world-building, the emotional beats—they echo Pixar and Universal so closely that ‘In Your Dreams’ struggles to form an identity of its own. It plays things safe, relies on familiar formulas, and avoids taking creative risks, resulting in a film that is pleasant enough but hardly memorable.

The music is one of the film’s clear wins. Tracks like The Eurythmics’ “Sweet Dreams” and the two Sandman numbers—a cover of the 1954 Chordettes classic and a sample from Metallica’s iconic track—are used smartly. They tap into the parents’ nostalgia while still landing well with younger viewers, creating a soundtrack that feels familiar, fun, and well-placed within the dreamscape.

In the end, ‘In Your Dreams’ is a harmless, feel-good watch–it’s warm, visually pleasant, and sprinkled with enough heart to keep you engaged, even if it never breaks new ground. For anyone hoping for something fresh or inventive, it may feel too familiar. But as a simple bedtime-style adventure about love, loss, and holding a family together, it lands softly—and that’s enough.

news.n_title: Dhoom 2 review: Pleasant rom-com that never achieves its full potential
news.n_description: Dhoom 2 review: Director Shanmuga Priyan's 'Love Marriage', starring Vikram Prabhu, Sushmitha Bhat and Meenakshi Dinesh, is a Tamil remake of the Telugu film, 'Ashoka Vanamlo Arjuna Kalyanam'. The pleasant romantic comedy is a perfect canvas for a laughter riot but bogged down by redundant ideas.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
When Indian cinema is dominated by action dramas with a newfound love for extreme violence and gore, there’s always something soothing about heartwarming dramas. Be it a simple love story or a family drama or a comedy drama, they remind you of the good old times. Director Shanmuga Priyan’s ‘Love Marriage’, a remake of the Telugu film, ‘Ashoka Vanmulo Arjuna Kalyanam', has all the elements to be that cutesy film that will leave a smile on your face. Did it succeed in its mission? Let’s find out!

Ramachandran (Vikram Prabhu) is a 33-year-old bachelor who is waiting to get married. A thorough gentleman, the only progressive man in a family of regressive people, goes through arranged marriage. He, along with his family and extended family, travels to a different town to meet a potential bride, which is followed by his engagement. One meeting is all it takes for Ram to get engaged to Ambika (Sushmitha Bhat) old-school style.

As he tries to strike up a conversation with Ambika, she resists, which instils doubts about his decision. However, the Covid-19 lockdown forces Ram’s family to stay at Ambika’s home. This situation leads Ramchandran to deal with the struggles of managing his budding relationship with Ambika, along with tackling regressive family members, who criticise every move of the bride and her family.

Director Shanmuga Priyan’s ‘Love Marriage’ has all the ingredients to make a fun-filled family drama that gives a glimpse into the struggles of an arranged marriage. Ram’s parents see him as a burden as he has crossed the limit of what is considered as a marriageable age. They agree to find a bride belonging to a different caste. There’s a relative who keeps cribbing about everything and another who indulges in alcohol on every occasion.

From the bride’s side, there’s a non-stop jabbermouth uncle and a cute bride’s sister to make things interesting. Yet, with all these quirky characters, the film doesn’t achieve its full potential. The first half of the film treads smoothly because it doesn’t take itself seriously. Though predictable, the story moves forward with the gradual introduction of conflicts, which makes one wonder what’s next. But, when the film starts taking itself seriously, it falls prey to redundancy and outdated ideas.

With a taut runtime of two hours, ‘Love Marriage’ still feels stretched because of its overdramatic and convenient turn of events in the second half. The whole stretch featuring Vikram Prabhu’s Ramachandran and Meenakshi Dinesh’s ‘Radha’ feels inorganic and forced. However, the film gets the message right - especially when Ram talks about how society pressures people to live and lead a life based on a timeline that everyone follows.

Vikram Prabhu is perfect as Ramachandran and the way he portrays the stages of getting to know a girl to becoming her partner is enjoyable. Ambika, as the coy bride, expresses with her eyes. Meenakshi Dinesh looks effervescent in a role that’s quite cliched. The film could have done away with the songs and an unfunny cameo by a veteran actor, which added nothing to the story. Ramesh Thilak, Gajaraj and Aruldoss played supporting roles to the best of their abilities.

Cinematographer Madhan Christopher’s frames show the goodness of the village, while Sean Roldan’s music, though soothing, feels jarring at moments.

‘Love Marriage’ is a film that could have become a complete laughter riot given its potential. Nonetheless, it ends up as a predictable family film that reminds you of the early 2000s.

news.n_title: Movie Review: Vikrant Massey film seems like an extended Instagram reel
news.n_description: Blackout movie review: The Vikrant Masey film is all over the place with multiple characters and subplots. It never gets a chance to swim smoothly through and makes for an exhausting watch.

n_offline_data.no_desc_withouthtml:
What do you say about a film where most of the audience in the theatre is busy on their phones? Blackout, starring Vikrant Massey and Sunil Grover, did start on a promising note but fell flat on its face, even before we could settle comfortably into our seats. The film is chaotic and loud, and even when sprinkled with some forced jokes, it remains unfunny. If the JioCinema film is to be described colloquially, it would be 'Kuch Bhi'!

Blackout looks like a heist drama as the credits roll out, but soon it turns out to be a story of a common man, Lenny Dsouza (Massey), and how a night changes his life. There is a city-wide blackout, an accident, an unguarded treasure, a dead body to dispose of, a drunkard and two pickpockets as aides, a bitchy boss, a Byomkesh Bakshi-styled detective, a damsel in distress, betrayal from loved ones, ex-MLA up for revenge, a police officer waiting to attack, gang wars, guns and bullets, a henchman, and a lot of confusion.

Isn't that just too much, not only for Lenny but also for the audience? There are times when you feel the film is an extended version of social media videos where there are absurd twists to evoke laughter. Everything that Lenny encounters goes wrong, pulling him further into a murkier mess.

Earlier this year, we saw audiences enjoying slapstick comedy in Madgaon Express. In the past, we have also seen films like Delhi Belly, Kaalakaandi, and Go Goa Gone, with the same theme. The humour in Blackout, however, seems forced. While there are a few genuinely good jokes by an influencer duo, they are never highlighted.

The film only manages to tickle the funny bone towards the last 10 minutes when all loose ends are tied. (Spoiler alert!) There’s a scene where Vikrant’s character shoots bullets out of a gun while SRK’s ‘Chaiyya Chaiyya’ plays out. That was the star gag of the film.

Coming to performances, Vikrant Massey tries hard to play a loud Punekar. Given how he's aced most of his characters in the past, this one fails to make an impact. His portrayal as the sting operation journalist also looks unconvincing.

 
 
 
 
View this post on Instagram
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

A post shared by Vikrant Massey (@vikrantmassey)

Sunil Grover's Bewda was written with Shah Rukh Khan's Pathaan and Jawan in mind. From his long hair look to his action sequence in the climax, Sunil embodies the superstar in most scenes. There's also a reference to 'Badshah' but we leave it for you to discover. The comedian plays an alcoholic street dweller with a past who is also termed 'sasta Ghalib' for mouthing tragic poems all along.

There’s a dialogue in the film where a character, amused with so many people walking into Lenny's life, says how no one would be paid in full as they would be tagged as cameos. And that seems to be the reality of Blackout.

The makers got star performers like Jisshu Sengupta, Chhaya Kadam, Mouni Roy, and Anant Joshi, to play minuscule roles in the films. And that was truly heartbreaking. These actors never got a chance to bite their teeth into these poorly fleshed characters. Social media influencers Karan Sonawane and Saurabh Ghadge, who play Thik Thak, however, manage to add some light humour with their banters. The voiceover by Anil Kapoor, in his inimitable style, was also a treat to hear.

The director of the film, Devang Shashin Bhavsar, struck gold with the casting, but when you have a weak script, nothing works. Also, even though the basic theme had potential, the filmmaker did a shoddy job of setting the right narrative tone and making the plot engaging.

The film is all over the place with multiple characters and subplots. It never gets a chance to swim smoothly through and makes for an exhausting watch. The music by Vishal Mishra is also quite forgettable though using classic Bollywood songs at multiple junctures was a masterstroke. Those are probably the only moments when the happenings on the screen got everyone’s attention.

Blackout is streaming on JioCinema.
